title: Assignment 4
text:
1. Strong v. Weak Entity Set
A strong entity set is an entity set that has a primary key of its own, meaning its entities can be uniquely identified by their own attributes alone
A weak entity set’s existence depends on another entity (i.e. identifying entity) and does not have a primary key of its own. Instead, it’s identified using the primary key of the strong entity plus a special attribute called a discriminator

title: Assignment 4
text:
3a. Appending natural join
Appending natural join section does not change the result because, as defined in Chapter 4, a natural join matches tuples on all common attributes and retains only one copy of each common column.
The relation takes contains the attributes (course_id, sec_id, semester, year), which form a foreign key referencing the primary key of section. By referential integrity, every tuple in takes must already match a tuple in section ch4 . Therefore, performing natural join section neither removes nor adds tuples—it only adds additional attributes that are not used in the SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, or HAVING clauses. Hence, the result of the query remains unchanged.

text:

FROM employee NATURAL LEFT OUTER JOIN manages
WHERE manager_id IS NULL;

title: Assignment 1
text:
Database Systems
iPERMS (Interactive Personnel Electronic Records Management System)
A database system used to store and access data related to individual service members, such as service history, evaluations, training records, and official documents. The database allows authorized users to retrieve, update, and maintain records over time, allowing secure access to personnel information across different units and assignments.
Workday
A human resources application that employs a database system to store and manage employee data such as personal information, job roles, payroll details, benefits enrollment, and performance records.The database allows authorized users and employees to access and update information over time.
Notion
A productivity app that uses a database system to store and manage persistent data such as notes, task lists, project boards, and user-created tables. The database allows users to create, update, and retrieve structured information across sessions and devices, ensuring data is persistently stored. Notion’s use of relational tables and linked databases supports flexible data organization.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Domain Project Proposal 1
Public Benefit Access Database
Purpose: To analyze accessibility of public benefits (housing assistance, food aid, unemployment services) across different demographic and geographic groups to identify gaps and inequities.


Functions:

Store benefit programs and eligibility rules
Track application volume and approval rates
Associate benefits with ZIP codes or census tracts
Compare access across demographic indicators
Identify under-served areas


User Interface:
Program directory: Browse available benefit programs
Application view: Displays participation and approval trends
Geographic comparison table: Compare differences across locations
Equity analysis dashboard: Visualize disparities in access and outcomes.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Domain Project Proposal 2
Litigation Outcomes and Risk Analysis Database
Purpose: To analyzes publicly available court case data to identify litigation trends and potential legal risk patterns across jurisdictions.


Functions:

Store publicly available case metadata (case type, jurisdiction, etc.)
Record case outcomes using simplified categorical labels
Track case duration and resolution patterns
Compare litigation trends across courts and case types
Support identification of areas with higher litigation frequency or risk


User Interface

Case Directory: Browse and filter cases by jurisdiction, case type, and status
Outcome Summary View: Display aggregated case outcome trends
Jurisdiction Comparison Table: Compare litigation patterns across courts Risk Analysis
Dashboard: Visualize high-level trends in case volume, duration, and outcomes

title: Assignment 1
text:
Domain Project Proposal 3
Small Business Stability Database
Purpose: Design and implement a database that analyzes local economic conditions affecting small business stability and resilience over time


Functions:

Store small business characteristics (industry type, size, location)
Track business openings and closures over time
Record economic indicators such as unemployment rates and inflation periods
Associate business trends with geographic areas (ZIP codes or census tracts)
Compare business stability across industries and locations


User Interface

Business Directory: Browse businesses by industry and location Economic Indicators
View: Display selected economic trends over time
Geographic Comparison Table: Compare business openings and closures across areas
Stability Analysis Dashboard: Visualize business survival and closure patterns

title: Assignment 1
text:
Data Mining
Data mining allows data interpretation to go beyond table formatting. It allows developers to find patterns and correlations using databases, machine learning, statistical analysis, and most recently AI. It allows organizations to find hidden trends, bottlenecks, and other insights which can serve as tools to solve multiple challenges.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Social Media Information Storage
Comment Table
The comment relation stores comments made by users on a post. The attributes include comment_id as the primary key, user_id as a foreign key referencing the User relation, and post_id as a foreign key referencing the Post relation, the comment text, and time stamp.
Follow Table
The follow relation stores information about the relationship between users, such as when one user follows another. The attributes include follower_id, and followed_id, which are both foreign keys referencing the user relation.
Message Table
The message relation stores private messages sent between users on the social media platform. Attributes include message_id as the primary key, sender_id and receiver_id as foreign keys referencing the user relation, the message content, and time stamp.

title: Assignment 2
text:
Relation Schema, Relation, and Instance
A relation schema consists of a list of attributes and their corresponding domains. For example, the relation schema of department is:
department (dept_name, building. budget)
The term relation is used to refer to a table. A relation has multiple rows called tuples and columns called attributes. For example, the prereq relation will have a course_id attribute and a prereq_id attribute with multiple tuples showing the ID numbers.
An instance refers to a specific instance of a relation containing a specific set of rows. For example: the instance of instructor has 12 tuples corresponding to 12 instructors

title: Assignment 2
text: Bank Database Schema

title: Assignment 2
text:
AI for databases
AI can clean data by detecting missing values, incorrect data, and inconsistent formats. AI can detect unusual patterns automticaly while traditional methods rely on fixed rules. Not only can it detect, but it can also suggest corrections based on context. The same can be done with duplicate records. AI can learn patterns from the data to detect duplicates, while traditional methods require manually defined rules.
Large Language Models also allows user to query a database using plain English instead of writing SQL code. LLM’s can analyze the database schema and map natural language phrases to schema alerts to generate SQL. Traditional methods require users to understand SQL syntax and schema structure, but AI requires no prior experience and can even create query’s faster with fewer mistakes.

title: Assignment 5
section: 3. Why do we need weak entity sets?
text:
3. Why do we need weak entity sets?
Weak entity sets are used because they represent entities whose existence depends on another entity and whose identity is determined using the key of that identifying entity along with a discriminator. Using weak entities allows the database design to model real-world dependencies clearly without introducing artificial keys.
Ex: Section depends on course. The attributes of section alone are not enough to uniquely identify it, so section needs to be identified using course_id.

title: Assignment 5
text:
4a
Employee lives in same city as the company they work for
SELECT e.ID, e.person_name
FROM employee AS e
JOIN works   AS w ON e.ID = w.ID
JOIN company AS c ON w.company_name = c.company_name
WHERE e.city = c.city;
Employee lives in same city and same street as their manager
SELECT e.ID, e.person_name
FROM employee AS e
JOIN manages  AS m   ON e.ID = m.ID
JOIN employee AS mgr ON m.manager_id = mgr.ID
WHERE e.city = mgr.city
  AND e.street = mgr.street;
Employee earns more than the average salary of employees at their company
SELECT e.ID, e.person_name
FROM employee AS e
JOIN works AS w ON e.ID = w.ID
WHERE w.salary >
      (SELECT AVG(w2.salary)
       FROM works AS w2
       WHERE w2.company_name = w.company_name);

title: Assignment 5
text:
4b
The query is wrong because natural join joins tables using all attributes with the same name. In this case, it incorrectly joins section and course on both course_id and title. While the relationship should only use course_id, it’s also using title because both section and course contain that attribute. in order for it to work, the schema needs to explicitly specify the join condition.
